old_claim.note: Steam Engine supports manufacturing, deployment, commercialization, or operational scaling.

new_claim.note: Mont Cenis compressed-air machinery used water power, hydraulic compressors, reservoirs, and pressure pipes to generate and distribute air for the drills.

source_shape.source_locator: Drinker describes Colladon's proposal to apply compressed air as a motor using mountain streams for compression, then details the reservoirs, iron pipes, water-driven compressors, air tanks, and pressure pipes used around Mont Cenis.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source grounds the Mont Cenis compressed-air system in water-powered air compression and pressure distribution infrastructure.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports replacing the steam-engine edge because the first practical tunnel-drilling system used hydraulic water power to compress and distribute air, not steam power as its direct prerequisite.

ontology_before: The node depended on Steam Engine, implying steam power was the direct scaling foundation for early compressed-air tools.
ontology_after: The node depends on Hydraulics because the source-backed Mont Cenis system used water-powered compressors, reservoirs, and pressure pipes.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: pneumatic_tools_compressed_air_power now depends on hydraulics and no longer directly depends on steam_engine.
would_reject_if: The node were rescoped to steam-driven workshop air compressors rather than the Mont Cenis compressed-air system.
A stronger source showed the first practical compressed-air drilling system required steam engines instead of hydraulic compressors.
The hydraulics node were narrowed away from fluid-power control and water-pressure machinery.
short_reason: The first practical compressed-air scope used water-powered compression, not direct steam power.
